en.extract:
The maned owl (Jubula lettii) or the Akun scops owl, is a species of owl in the family Strigidae that is endemic to Africa. It is the only species in genus Jubula.


Description
The maned owl gets its name from the long, floppy, white-tipped feathers on its crown and ear tufts which seem to form a mane. Otherwise it is a medium-sized, large-headed, rufous owl. The upperparts are deep chestnut with narrow, wavy dark bars, which are darker on the lesser wing coverts and on the head, so that they contrast with the white forehead and tips to the ear-tufts. The facial disk is rufous with a broad blackish rim and a white throat. The upper breast is rufous with white bars, the lower breast and belly are buff with dark streaks while the vent and legs are pale buff. The flight feathers and the tail have broad dark bars on a rufous background. The bill is yellow, the cere greenish-yellow and the eyes, legs and feet are yellow. The body length is 25 cm (9.8 in).


Distribution and habitat
The maned owl is known only from the lowland, tropical rainforest of west Africa and has a patchy distribution from Liberia in the west through to the Democratic Republic of Congo.  The patchiness of its distribution is shown by the countries it has been recorded in which are Liberia, Ivory Coast, Ghana, Cameroon, Equatorial Guinea, Gabon, Congo and the Democratic Republic of Congo.
The maned owl seems to prefer closed canopy evergreen rainforest, especially areas with dense creepers, it is not found in semi-evergreen open-canopy forest, and there are no records away from dense forest or forest clearings.


Behaviour
The maned owl is not well known. It is nocturnal, roosting by day in dense creepers as close to the ground as 2 m (6.6 ft), emerging out onto an open perch at dusk. Their diet is not well understood, but small and weak feet and claws suggest that insects such as grasshoppers and beetles form the largest part of its diet. Green plant material has been found in the stomach contents of a young owl.
As with its diet little is known of its breeding behaviour and the call is unrecorded. In the Democratic Republic of the Congo a nest was observed and the results suggest that this species lays three or four eggs between March and May. The nest is in a tree cavity or an old stick nest of another animal is reused. Juveniles have been recorded in late December and January in Cameroon and Gabon, and a pair of adults with full-grown young were observed in Liberia in late February.


References

The '''maned owl''' ('''''Jubula lettii''''') or the '''Akun scops owl''', is a species of [[owl]] in the family [[Strigidae]] that is endemic to [[Africa]]. It is the [[Monotypic taxon|only species]] in [[genus]] '''''Jubula'''''.<ref name = Avibase>{{cite web | url = http://avibase.bsc-eoc.org/species.jsp?avibaseid=15A1DBE877AB2E8A | title = Maned Owl ''Jubula lettii'' (Büttikofer, 1889) | access-date = 26 October 2016 | publisher = Denis Lepage|work = Avibase}}</ref>

==Description==
The maned owl gets its name from the long, floppy, white-tipped feathers on its crown and ear tufts which seem to form a mane. Otherwise it is a medium-sized, large-headed, rufous owl. The upperparts are deep chestnut with narrow, wavy dark bars, which are darker on the lesser wing coverts and on the head, so that they contrast with the white forehead and tips to the ear-tufts. The facial disk is rufous with a broad blackish rim and a white throat. The upper breast is rufous with white bars, the lower breast and belly are buff with dark streaks while the vent and legs are pale buff. The flight feathers and the tail have broad dark bars on a rufous background. The bill is yellow, the [[cere]] greenish-yellow and the eyes, legs and feet are yellow. The body length is {{convert|25|cm|in|abbr=on}}.<ref name = Kemp>{{cite book | last1 = Kemp | first1 = Alan | last2 = Kemp |first2 = Meg | year = 1998 |  title = Birds of Prey of Africa and its Islands | pages = 286–287 | publisher = New Holland | isbn =1-85974-100-2}}</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==
The maned owl is known only from the lowland, tropical rainforest of west Africa and has a patchy distribution from [[Liberia]] in the west through to the [[Democratic Republic of Congo]].  The patchiness of its distribution is shown by the countries it has been recorded in which are Liberia, [[Ivory Coast]], [[Ghana]], [[Cameroon]], [[Equatorial Guinea]], [[Gabon]], [[Republic of Congo|Congo]] and the Democratic Republic of Congo.<ref name = Kemp/><ref name="iucn status 16 November 2021">{{cite iucn |author=BirdLife International |date=2016 |title=''Jubula lettii'' |volume=2016 |article-number=e.T22689173A93220733 |doi=10.2305/IUCN.UK.2016-3.RLTS.T22689173A93220733.en |access-date=16 November 2021}}</ref>

The maned owl seems to prefer closed canopy evergreen rainforest, especially areas with dense creepers, it is not found in semi-evergreen open-canopy forest, and there are no records away from dense forest or forest clearings.<ref name="iucn status 16 November 2021" />

==Behaviour==
The maned owl is not well known. It is nocturnal, roosting by day in dense creepers as close to the ground as {{convert|2|m|ft|abbr=on}}, emerging out onto an open perch at dusk.<ref name = Kemp/> Their diet is not well understood, but small and weak feet and claws suggest that insects such as grasshoppers and beetles form the largest part of its diet. Green plant material has been found in the stomach contents of a young owl.

As with its diet little is known of its breeding behaviour and the call is unrecorded. In the Democratic Republic of the Congo a nest was observed and the results suggest that this species lays three or four eggs between March and May. The nest is in a tree cavity or an old stick nest of another animal is reused.<ref name = Avibirds>{{cite web | url = http://www.avibirds.com/html/owls/Maned_Owl.html#.WBCarqPIYyI | title = Profile Maned Owl | access-date = 26 October 2016 | publisher = Avibirds}}</ref> Juveniles have been recorded in late December and January in Cameroon and Gabon, and a pair of adults with full-grown young were observed in Liberia in late February.<ref name=arkive>{{cite web | url = http://www.arkive.org/maned-owl/jubula-lettii/ | title = Maned owl (''Jubula lettii'') | access-date = 16 October 2016 | publisher = Wildscreen | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20160805120313/http://www.arkive.org/maned-owl/jubula-lettii/ | archive-date = 2016-08-05 }}</ref>

zh.extract:
鬃鴞（學名Jubula lettii）是非洲一種貓頭鷹，是鬃鴞屬下的唯一物種。
鬃鴞分佈在喀麥隆、中非共和國、剛果共和國、剛果民主共和國、科特迪瓦、赤道畿內亞、加蓬、加納及利比里亞。
